attributes.stem: A 27-year-old man presents to his General Practitioner (GP) complaining of a six-week history of worsening pain and swelling in his left knee and right index finger. The pain is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. He does not remember an incident or trauma at the time of onset. He does not suffer from any back pain or any other painful, stiff or swollen joints. He has no significant medical history except for chronic mild skin irritation and dandruff. He takes no regular medications. On examination, the entire right index finger is swollen and erythematous. The left knee is mildly swollen with no erythema.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This presentation of oligoarthritis is most likely psoriatic arthritis. Psoriatic arthritis is an entity within the group of seronegative spondyloarthropathies which share common genetic and clinical features - musculoskeletal (arthritis, enthesitis, dactylitis) and non-musculoskeletal (cutaneous psoriasis, iritis, inflammatory bowel disease). Classical features of psoriatic arthritis are joint pain and stiffness, particularly early morning stiffness lasting >30 minutes and dactylitis (uniform swelling of an entire digit). Patients may also have features of cutaneous psoriasis including a rash described classically as an erythematous plaque-like raised rash typically on the extensor surfaces, plus scalp (dandruff) and nail (pitting, onycholysis, ridging, crumbling and discolouration) involvement.

It can be distinguished from rheumatoid arthritis due to the presence of dactylitis, scalp and nail involvement and distal interphalangeal joint involvement. Hence, as this patient has evidence of dactylitis and scalp involvement, rheumatoid arthritis is not the best fit in this case.

Gout, or the deposition of urate crystal into the joint space is unlikely to be the correct answer in this case. Typically, gout will not improve with movement (will cause significant pain) and the timeframe is unlikely to match here.

Osteoarthritis is a condition in which there is a failure to maintain balance between cartilage synthesis and degradation. Prevalence of osteoarthritis increases with age, particularly in those over 50 years. It typically presents with pain associated with activities, early morning stiffness <30min in duration, joint effusion, joint locking and crepitus, reduced range of motion and deformity such as joint malalignment. Given this patient's age, presence of dactylitis and features indicative of psoriasis, this diagnosis is unlikely.

Septic arthritis is an orthopaedic emergency that needs to be kept in mind for all presentations of arthritis. The presentation is often of one or more inflamed joints that are erythematous, highly painful, warm and swollen on a background of infection elsewhere and/or IVDU (haematogenous inoculation) or recent trauma or surgery (direct inoculation). This does not fit the time course of symptoms in this case and would not improve with movement throughout the day as described above.
learningPoints: Patients with psoriasis are at risk of development of psoriatic arthritis.
references: Brockbank J, Gladman D. Diagnosis and management of psoriatic arthritis. Drugs. 2002;62(17):2447-57. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12421102/
Pujalte GG, Albano-Aluquin SA. Differential Diagnosis of Polyarticular Arthritis. Am Fam Physician. 2015;92(1):35-41. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26132125/
